reasoning: The CDC updated its AMR mortality estimates in 2013 (23k deaths) and 2019 (35k deaths). While a new report could potentially be released in 2025 (following the 6-year pattern), reaching 70,000 deaths would require a 100% increase over the current 35k estimate (or a 45% increase if C. diff is included to reach 48k). Although the COVID-19 pandemic caused roughly a 15% spike in hospital-onset AMR infections, this natural growth falls far short of doubling the baseline. The only plausible path to 70,000+ deaths would be a radical methodological shift—such as counting deaths "associated" with rather than strictly "attributable" to AMR (similar to the alternative 160,000+ estimate by Burnham et al. or the Lancet GRAM global methodology). However, recent U.S. National Action Plan reports and 2025 CDC advisory council documents consistently continue to cite the 35,000 figure. Without any documented indication of an imminent methodological overhaul by the CDC or another major US source, the probability of hitting the 70,000 threshold by December 2025 remains very low.
